article_title: AstraZeneca's Eneboparatide Frees 31% of Hypoparathyroidism Patients From Calcium and Vitamin D at Week 24 in CALYPSO Phase III
summary: All five snapshots reachable; trial-readout specifics are correct but attributed in the article to Pharmaceutical Executive — they actually come from the AstraZeneca release as published via Mirage News (also cited). A corrections record will document the misattribution.

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: AstraZeneca's Eneboparatide Frees 31% of Hypoparathyroidism Patients From Calcium and Vitamin D at Week 24 in CALYPSO Phase III
content_preview.summary: AstraZeneca's CALYPSO Phase III hit its composite primary endpoint with 31.1% of eneboparatide patients normalizing serum calcium and dropping supplements at week 24 versus 5.9% on placebo, with benefits sustained through a 52-week extension.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

AstraZeneca's investigational rare-disease drug eneboparatide met the composite primary endpoint of its CALYPSO Phase III trial in adults with chronic hypoparathyroidism, with 31.1% of treated patients reaching a normal blood-calcium range while dropping their oral calcium and active vitamin D supplements at week 24, compared with 5.9% on placebo, [Pharmaceutical Executive reported](https://www.pharmexec.com/view/phase-iii-trial-data-show-eneboparatide-normalizes-serum-calcium-level...

recommendations: File the corrections record at src/content/corrections/2026-05/13-astrazenecas-eneboparatide-...-calypso-phase-iii.json documenting that the late-breaker specifics attributed to Pharmaceutical Executive in the body actually come from the AstraZeneca release as published via Mirage News.
Bot reminder: when a story has a year-old topline and a fresh late-breaker readout, the specific late-breaker numbers must be attributed to the late-breaker source, not the topline coverage.

editor_notes.content_quality: Solid biotech News piece, 872 words. Strong coverage of the trial design, the composite primary endpoint, the safety and durability data, and the Amolyt-acquisition backstory. The 'What We Don't Know' section is editorially honest about regulatory-filing timing and the limits of a 202-patient pivotal trial.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all five reachable snapshots. source-0 (Mirage News carrying the AstraZeneca press release) confirms ALL the specific trial-readout numbers verbatim: 202 patients, 12 countries, 2:1 ratio, composite primary endpoint definition (8.3–10.6 mg/dL albumin-adjusted sCa + independence from active vitamin D + ≤600 mg/day oral calcium), 31.1% vs 5.9% (n=41/132 vs n=4/68; p=0.0001), 52-week extension, BMD T- and Z-score safety framing, ECE Prague May 12, Gianluca Pirozzi attribution. source-1 (Pharmaceutical Executive, March 18, 2025) is an EARLIER topline-readout article, NOT the May 2026 ECE late-breaker. It confirms 202 patients, 2:1 ratio, Fast Track + Orphan Drug FDA designations, Marc Dunoyer quote, primary endpoint defined in general terms — but does NOT contain the 31.1% / 5.9% / 41/132 / 4/68 / p=0.0001 / 8.3–10.6 / 600 mg specific numbers, which are the May 2026 late-breaker results. **The article attributes the specific numbers in its 'What We Know' section to Pharmaceutical Executive ('per Pharmaceutical Executive') — that attribution is incorrect. The numbers themselves are correct (they appear verbatim in source-0).** source-2 (Clinical Trials Arena) confirms eneboparatide=AZP-3601, mechanism (PTH type 1 receptor agonist, conformation-specific), and Fast Track + Orphan Drug FDA + EMA Orphan designation. source-3 (Pharmaceutical Executive March 2024 acquisition story) confirms Alexion rare-disease unit framing. source-4 (GlobeNewswire AstraZeneca 2024 close release) confirms $800M upfront + $250M contingent / closed 15 July 2024.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific NUMERICAL fact is correct and verifiable in the cited sources collectively — but the misattribution to Pharmaceutical Executive of the May 2026 late-breaker specifics is a citation error worth a corrections record. The substance (31.1%, 5.9%, 41/132, 4/68, p=0.0001, 8.3-10.6 mg/dL, 600 mg, 52 weeks, 12 countries, Prague ECE) is accurate; the cite is wrong.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, scientifically careful. Appropriate caveats on durability beyond one year and on the binary nature of the composite endpoint.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of eneboparatide or CALYPSO.
concerns: Citation misattribution: the article cites Pharmaceutical Executive (an older March 2025 topline article) for the specific May 2026 ECE late-breaker numbers, which actually come from the AstraZeneca release as published via Mirage News (also cited in the article). The substance is correct; the attribution is wrong.
recommendations: File the corrections record documenting the misattribution.
Bot reminder: cross-check the date of each source against the specific claim being attributed. A March 2025 article cannot be the source of May 2026 readout numbers.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. The headline / summary / lead are substantively correct (the 31.1% efficacy figure is real and verified). The misattribution of the late-breaker numbers to Pharmaceutical Executive instead of the AstraZeneca-via-Mirage-News release is a citation error that a single corrections record can honestly cover. The reader is still informed accurately about the trial; they just need to know which source actually carried which numbers.
